title: Apple tarte Tatin
subtitle: Caramelised apples baked under puff pastry, inverted
excerpt: The French inverted apple tart: apples cooked in butter and sugar to a deep caramel in an ovenproof pan, topped with puff pastry, baked, and turned out so the caramelised apples are on top. One 23 cm Tatin, serving six to eight.
sourceNotes: The Tatin sisters of the Hôtel Tatin in Lamotte-Beuvron (late nineteenth century) are credited with the inverted tart's accidental creation; the form became established in French household cookery in the early twentieth century. Marie Ébrard, La Cuisinière de la campagne et de la ville (pre-1928, public domain in France): documents the caramel-apples-and-pastry technique. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): notes the inverted apple tart as a French method recognised in British households.

recipe.freezeNotes: Tarte Tatin is best fresh; the apples weep on defrost.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Caramelise the apples and rest in the pan up to 2 hours before topping with pastry and baking.

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm, oven-proof — or a heavy ovenproof frying pan.
For peeling and coring the apples.

glossaryTerms.definition: The caramel for a Tatin is made directly in the pan that bakes the tart. Sugar is melted with butter over medium heat until deep amber; the apple halves are arranged cut-side up in the hot caramel and cooked further until tender and slightly burnished. The pastry top is added in the pan; the whole thing goes into the oven; the tart is inverted onto a plate after a short rest. Burnt caramel turns bitter — pull the pan from the heat as soon as it hits a deep mahogany.

content.text: Tarte Tatin is built upside-down in a heavy ovenproof pan. Butter and sugar cook to a deep amber caramel; apple halves are pressed into the caramel cut-side up and cooked until they soften and burnish; a disc of puff pastry is laid over the apples and tucked down; the whole thing bakes until the pastry is risen and golden; the tart is inverted onto a serving plate so the apples are on top.

Use a firm dessert apple that holds its shape: Cox, Braeburn, or russet. Cooking apples collapse to apple sauce; eating apples that hold structure are correct here. A heavy pan with a metal handle is essential — the pan goes into the oven, and a plastic handle will melt.

attrs.title: Caramel is hot
attrs.body: The caramel reaches around 170°C. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

items.prepNote: about 6 medium Cox, Braeburn or russet — peeled, cored, halved
all-butter, rolled to a 25 cm round, 3 mm thick

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Squeeze the {{lemon}} over the {{apple-eating}} halves to stop them browning. Put the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} into the cast-iron pan over medium heat. Stir until the butter melts and the sugar starts to bubble. Cook 4–5 minutes more without stirring; swirl the pan; cook until the caramel turns deep amber. This is the
stage. Take off the heat. Arrange the apple halves cut-side up in a tight spiral in the caramel, packing them in.
Cook the apples, lay the pastry

Return to a medium heat for 15 minutes; the apples should soften and burnish on the underside. Lift the pan off the heat. Lay the rolled {{puff-pastry}} over the apples, tucking the edges down inside the pan around the fruit.

Bake for 25–30 minutes; the pastry should be deep gold and risen. Rest the pan on a heatproof surface for 10 minutes. Place a serving plate over the pan, hold both firmly, and turn the whole thing over in one steady movement. Lift the pan away; the apples should be glossy with caramel on top of the puff. Serve warm with crème fraîche or vanilla ice cream.
